topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery happened on a Tuesday afternoon while I was looking for the remote. Tucked behind a stack of well-worn textbooks on the shelf, I found a leather-bound journal. My breath hitched in my throat as I flipped it open. The script inside was undeniably mine—my mannerisms, the way I frowned when concentrating, the specific cadence of my casual observations. The words detailed my morning routine, the slight hesitation before I ordered coffee, the way I reacted to unexpected silence. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I stood there, the journal heavy in my hands, my fingers trembling slightly as I reread a passage describing a dream I’d had last week. When my partner walked in, humming off-key, I quickly slammed the book shut, my voice sounding unnaturally bright. "Just... organizing," I managed, my eyes fixed firmly on the doorway, avoiding their gaze.

[story 2]

It started as a misplaced bookmark, a stray page tucked into the spine of a book I thought belonged to my partner. Curiosity, that insidious little thief, pulled me to examine it. What I found wasn't a grocery list or a half-finished thought; it was prose. Pages and pages dedicated to my life, meticulously chronicled with an intimacy that felt invasive. Reading about my deepest insecurities articulated in such eloquent, detached language made the air feel thick and hard to draw in. I retreated to the living room, pacing the worn rug, my jaw tight. Every sound—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed amplified. When my partner finally approached, offering a casual greeting, I forced a smile that felt brittle on my face. "Oh, that old thing?" I asked, my tone clipped, my hands gripping my mug so tightly my knuckles were white.

[story 3]

I found the evidence accidentally while tidying up their desk. It wasn't a diary, but a collection of detailed character sketches, complete with dialogue snippets that mirrored conversations I’d had with friends, only viewed through a lens of profound, almost clinical observation. The realization hit me like a physical blow: they weren't just *thinking* about me; they were constructing me. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, making the room swim slightly. I backed away slowly, my movements becoming jerky, as if moving through water. When my partner finally noticed my sudden withdrawal, their expression shifted—a flicker of surprise quickly replaced by a practiced, easy calm. "What's wrong?" they asked, their voice smooth, too smooth. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry, and managed a weak, "Nothing. Just tired."
